metadata.title: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't Actually Show Cancer

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. What is it actually measuring — and when does that difference matter? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: What is it actually measuring — and when does that difference matter??
props.segment: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't

beats.narration_text: Three lymph nodes, lighting up bright on the PET scan. A surgical team moves to plan salvage radiation. The scan is technically correct. The diagnosis is completely wrong.

card.copy: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't Actually Show Cancer
card.sub: the signal is real — the cause is wrong

beats.narration_text: FDG-PET is the standard tool for staging cancer and checking for recurrence. Bright spots on the scan mean the tracer has accumulated. Surgeons and oncologists act on those bright spots every day.

props.headline: FDG-PET is the standard tool for staging cancer and checking for…

beats.scene_description: a printed PET scan coronal image pinned to newsprint ground — warm amber-bright lymph node uptake regions visible against dark background, editorial desaturated treatment; slow push-in toward the bright nodes
beats.image_prompt: B02, a printed FDG-PET scan coronal image pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, warm amber-colored hotspots of radiotracer uptake visible in the chest and neck region against dark background, desaturated editorial reproduction, flat print, three glowing nodes clearly visible
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] PET scan with bright nodes — push-in

beats.narration_text: A PET scan is used to detect cancer. The tracer isn't looking for cancer cells. What is it actually measuring — and when does that difference matter?

card.copy: A PET scan is used to detect cancer. The tracer isn't looking for cancer cells. What is it actually measuring — and when does that difference matter?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION on screen — gap formula

beats.narration_text: FDG stands for fluorodeoxyglucose — a radioactive version of glucose. Cells take it up through the same transporters they use for sugar, and trap it inside. The camera counts where the decay happens.

production_viz.label: FDG enters cells like glucose — then gets trapped
production_viz.mechanic: a simplified cell outline on the left; glucose molecules flow in from the right through a transporter slot; once inside they convert to a labeled 'FDG-trapped' marker in crimson; a decay-signal symbol (gamma rays) radiates from the cell
production_viz.note: establish the uptake mechanism before naming what it actually measures

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] FDG uptake — glucose enters, gets trapped

beats.narration_text: The PET camera doesn't see cancer. It sees glucose metabolism — specifically, hexokinase activity and glucose-transporter expression. Tumors glow because they burn glucose fast. The Warburg effect: cancer cells favor glycolysis even when oxygen is available.

production_viz.label: PET reads metabolism, not malignancy
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: LEFT 'what you think you see' — a tumor outline; RIGHT 'what PET actually reads' — a hexokinase activity meter. An arrow from the tumor column to the right column is crossed out in crimson; a new arrow from 'glucose metabolism' to the meter in teal shows the real path
production_viz.note: the core proxy distinction — the signal is correct; the interpretation is the error

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-column: what you think vs what PET reads

beats.narration_text: And glucose metabolism is not unique to cancer. Activated immune cells in an infection light up just as bright. A healing surgical site glows for weeks. Brown adipose tissue — cold-activated fat in the neck and chest — fires like a lymph node.

props.headline: And glucose metabolism is not unique to cancer. Activated immune cells…

card.copy: Glucose metabolism is not unique to cancer
card.sub: inflammation · healing wounds · brown adipose tissue

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section — three false-positive causes listed as sub

beats.narration_text: False negatives are equally real. Tumors with low glycolytic flux — well-differentiated thyroid cancers, some prostate cancers, mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas — may take up almost no FDG despite substantial tumor burden. A dark scan is not an empty scan.

document.quote: A dark scan is not an empty scan.
document.attribution: — the FDG-negative lesion is metabolically quiet, not absent

beats.scene_description: large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'dark' as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] a dark scan is not an empty scan

beats.narration_text: Every imaging signal is a proxy. It reports what the tumor tends to do — not the tumor itself. That's why every clinical imaging report says 'consistent with' or 'suspicious for', never 'confirms'. Imaging suggests. Biopsy confirms.

production_viz.label: imaging suggests — biopsy confirms
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal chain: SIGNAL -> PROXY -> BIOLOGY. The arrow from PROXY to BIOLOGY has a question mark. Below it, two teal chips: 'IMAGING: consistent with' and 'BIOPSY: confirms'. The distinction is the payoff.
production_viz.note: the implication — establishes the chain and why it exists

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] signal chain: imaging suggests, biopsy confirms

beats.narration_text: Here's an illustrative case. A 58-year-old patient — breast cancer surgery three months prior. FDG-PET for recurrence surveillance. Three lymph nodes light up in the chest, each with high uptake. The team flags them for salvage radiation.

props.headline: Here's an illustrative case. A 58-year-old patient — breast cancer surgery…

beats.scene_description: a printed case summary sheet or referral note on newsprint, annotated with three circled lymph node locations and 'high FDG uptake' notation — editorial, desaturated, slow pan across
beats.image_prompt: B09, a printed oncology referral note pinned to aged newsprint, handwritten annotations circling three lymph node locations labeled 'high FDG uptake', a small PET scan thumbnail in corner showing bright spots, desaturated editorial reproduction, flat print

beats.narration_text: On biopsy: all three are reactive lymph nodes. The patient had a mild upper-respiratory infection the week before the scan. No malignant cells in any of them. The scan was technically correct — it found high glucose metabolism. It just found the wrong cause. Numbers are illustrative.

production_viz.label: three nodes, high uptake — reactive, not malignant
production_viz.mechanic: three node symbols in a row, each with a bright uptake indicator in crimson. Below each, the biopsy result reveals in teal: 'REACTIVE · URI'. A serif label: 'all three — no malignant cells found'. An illustrative note in small mono type: '(illustrative case)'
production_viz.note: illustrative case — invented numbers, realistic scenario

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three nodes reveal as reactive — not malignant

beats.narration_text: The PET scan did its job. It faithfully reported elevated glucose metabolism in three lymph nodes. The error wasn't the scanner. It was the assumption that high metabolism means cancer.

document.quote: The scanner was right. The assumption was wrong.
document.attribution: — high metabolism is not cancer; it is what cancer tends to do

beats.scene_description: large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'assumption' as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] the scanner was right — the assumption was wrong

beats.narration_text: FDG-PET doesn't show cancer. It shows glucose metabolism — and cancer is one of many things that raise it. Every imaging signal is a proxy: it reports what tissue tends to do, not what it is. That's why imaging suggests and biopsy confirms.

card.copy: A PET scan measures metabolism, not malignancy — imaging suggests, biopsy confirms.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Every imaging signal is a proxy. It reports what the tumor tends to do — not the tumor itself. That's why every clinical imaging report says 'consistent with' or 'suspicious for', never 'confirms'. Imaging suggests. Biopsy confirms. Here's an illustrative case. A 58-year-old patient — breast cancer surgery three months prior. FDG-PET for recurrence surveillance. Three lymph nodes light up in the chest, each with high uptake. The team flags them for salvage radiation.

props.artifactHeading: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't Actually Show
artifactLines: And glucose metabolism is not unique to cancer.
Every imaging signal is a proxy.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't Actually Show Cancer] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
props.segment: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't

props.title: Why a Glowing PET Scan Doesn't Actually Show Cancer
props.subline: that's why imaging suggests and biopsy confirms
